sessions.title: Kickoff
sessions.set_the_stage:
Welcome. Look around this room. This is your cohort. The people sitting next to you are the ones you will check in with, demo to, and stand alongside on Demo Day.

Most workshops end the moment the room empties. This one starts there. The weekend is the launch pad. The four weeks after are where the deeper builds happen, on your own time, with weekly office hours to keep you moving. Demo Day is where you stand in front of the room and show what you shipped.

Thirty minutes to set the expectation, then we start building.

questions.title: What do you want to walk out with on Demo Day?
questions.body: Not a vague aspiration. A specific sentence. By Demo Day I will have a portfolio that demonstrates I can _______. Write it down. Keep it visible all six weeks.

questions.title: What is the one thing that could stop you?
questions.body: Be honest. Time pressure during exams? Confidence? A tool you have never touched? Knowing the obstacle now lets you ask for help before it blocks you.

build.title: The program shape
build.instructions: Two days in this room. Then Week 1 chatbot, Weeks 2 and 3 agent, Week 4 Demo Day prep. Office hours every Saturday, 90 minutes, this room or on Meet. WhatsApp group for between-session unblocks. Demo Day in Week 5.

build.title: The portfolio at the end
build.instructions: Six builds plus a Demo Day recording, all collected at one URL. AI app, website, marketing collaterals (audio, video, brochure), chatbot, automation agent. All live. All yours. The URL is what you share with recruiters, on LinkedIn, and during placements.

build.title: How the room runs
build.instructions: When you are stuck for more than fifteen minutes, raise your hand. Do not power through silently. The facilitator walks the room precisely to catch the silent stuck. The fastest way to slow yourself down is to refuse help.

sessions.title: Pick your idea
sessions.set_the_stage:
Ninety minutes to land on the one micro-venture you will build over the next six weeks.

Eight ideas are on the screen. Each one is a real micro-venture that you, a family member, or someone you know could actually run. You pick one. Then you make it yours.

By the end of this session you have one sheet of paper. Your Ideation sheet. WHO you are building for, WHAT their problem is, and WHAT your build will do for them. The Ideation sheet is the first thing on your portfolio.

questions.title: Which of the ideas on the screen pulls you in?
questions.body: Read the set in front of you. If none of them pull you, switch to another set. Notice which one you keep coming back to. That is usually the right one. Trust the pull.

questions.title: Whose face do you see when you read it?
questions.body: If you picked Rent a Tree, whose farm is it? Yours? A relative's? If you picked Tiffin Choice, who is the cook? Write the name. The face makes the idea real. It might even be your own.

techniques: Is the person who would run this venture someone you know, or could it be you?
Can you talk to them today and ask one question? If it is a family venture, write the family member's name.
Is the problem something they actually face, or something you imagined?
When in doubt, message or call them now. One honest answer beats an hour of guessing.
Can you show this person what you built over the six weeks?
If you cannot reach them at all, pick a different person, or a different idea.

prompts.title: Sharpen your idea
prompts.content:
I am a college student in India. Over the next six weeks I am going to build a small, real venture for one real person, using AI tools to build all of it (a simple web app, a website, and so on). To be clear, this is not "an AI product." AI is just how I build it fast. The venture has to stand on its own by solving a real problem for a real person.

Pressure-test my venture idea like a sharp, honest mentor, not a cheerleader. Search the real web for evidence where you can, give sources, use rupees and real Indian examples, and say clearly when something is a guess.

My idea:
- The one specific person it is for (a real human, not "students" or "people"): [...]
- The exact moment the problem bites them: [...]
- What they do today instead: [...]
- What I will build for that person, in one plain sentence: [...]

Work through all six, specific to my exact person and to India:

1. IS THE PROBLEM REAL. Find evidence that people like this person actually face this problem (forum posts, articles, Reddit, news, anything real). Then give me 3 blunt questions I can ask my person that reveal real pain, about what they did last time, not whether they "would use an app."

2. WHO ALREADY SOLVES THIS. Search and name 3 to 5 real things solving this in India today (apps, websites, local services, or the plain manual workaround), with links. For each: what they get right, and the one gap my small local version could own. Be concrete.

3. CAN A STUDENT BUILD IT WITH AI IN SIX WEEKS. I will build a single-page web app with AI tools, no payment gateway, no live tracking, no app store. What is the smallest version that still solves the core problem? Tell me plainly what to build, what to fake or do by hand, and what to cut.

4. THE MONEY ON ONE CUSTOMER. For one customer, one use: what would they realistically pay, and what does it cost ME to deliver (materials, delivery, my own time) in rupees? If the maths does not work on one customer, say so. (Building it with AI is nearly free. I mean the real-world cost of running the venture.)

5. WHY NOW. What has changed that makes this doable for a student today and not three years ago? If "why now" is weak, tell me.

6. THE RISKIEST ASSUMPTION. The single belief that, if it is wrong, kills this venture. And the fastest way I could test it this week with real people, not by guessing.

End with a 3-line verdict: build it as-is, build it if I change one thing (which), or swap it for a sharper version of the same idea. Be honest. I would rather hear it now than on Demo Day.

build.title: Read the idea cards on the screen
build.instructions: Eight micro-ventures are on screen. Read each one carefully. Each card tells you who it is for, the problem, and what the app does. If none fit, switch sets using the pills at the top.

build.title: Pick one and make it yours
build.instructions: Pick the one that pulls you in. Then make it specific to your life. Rent a Tree becomes your family's farm. Tiffin Choice becomes the cook you actually know. The card is a starting point. You make it real.

build.title: Write your Ideation sheet
build.instructions: One sheet. Top: WHO (the name of the person who will run this venture, even if that is you). Below: WHAT THEY STRUGGLE WITH (one sentence). Below: WHAT MY BUILD WILL DO (three lines, plain English).

build.title: Share with the room
build.instructions: In the last few minutes, each founder reads their sheet aloud. Two sentences: who you are building for, and what you are building. The room listens.

sessions.title: Build your app
sessions.set_the_stage:
You have an idea and a user. Now you build the real thing.

Today you build a working product for your venture. Not a slide, not a mockup. A real page on the internet that shows what you offer, lets a customer place an order, emails you the moment it comes in, saves every order to a database, and gives you a dashboard to see them all.

You do not write the code. Gemini does. You tell it about your venture in plain words, paste in your own details, and put the pieces together one layer at a time. Tiffin, plants, sarees, tutoring, it does not matter what you sell. The steps are the same.

We go in order: set up your tools, build the order page, add the email, save to the database, build the dashboard, test it on your laptop, then put it live. By the end you have a real web address you can send to a real customer.

Follow the steps on screen. The exact prompts to copy are on the prompts page on your laptop. When you get stuck, the fix-it prompt is there for you.

prompts.title: Build your order page
prompts.desc: Creates index.html: shows your offerings, lets a customer choose, and place an order.
prompts.content:
I am building a simple website for my small venture. It is not an AI product. I am just using you to write the code for me. I will host it as one plain HTML file, with no backend.

Here is my venture:
- What I sell: [one line, for example home tiffins, rented plants, handloom sarees, exam tutoring]
- Who buys it: [one line, my customer]
- My offerings, one per line as Name | photo link or leave blank | short description or details like nutrition, size, material | price in rupees:
  [Name] | [photo link or blank] | [description or details] | [price]
  [Name] | [photo link or blank] | [description or details] | [price]
  (add as many as you have)
- If my offerings are grouped, the grouping is: [by day, by collection, by plan, or none]

Build me one file called index.html that does this:
- Shows my venture name and a one line tagline at the top.
- Shows each offering as a card with its name, its photo (use the link if I gave one, otherwise a neat placeholder I can swap later), its description or details, and its price. Group them if I said so.
- Lets the customer add an offering to their order and choose a quantity.
- Shows a running list of what they picked and the total in rupees.
- Has a short form for the customer name, email, and phone number, and an optional note.
- Has one clear button labelled Place order, not Submit.
- After they place the order, shows a friendly confirmation on the screen with their order summary.

Keep it clean and easy to read on a phone. Simple colours, nothing flashy. Put all my offerings near the top of the file so I can edit them easily later. No payment, no login, no backend yet. Do not add email or database steps yet, I will ask for those next. Give me the full index.html.

prompts.title: Add the email notification
prompts.desc: Wires EmailJS in so you get an email the moment an order comes in.
prompts.content:
Here is my current index.html:

[paste your full index.html]

I want an email to reach me every time a customer places an order. I am using EmailJS. From my EmailJS account I have:
- Public key: [paste]
- Service ID: [paste]
- Template ID: [paste]

My EmailJS template uses these fields: customer_name, customer_email, customer_phone, order_details, order_total, customer_note.

Add EmailJS to my page so that when the customer taps Place order, it sends me an email with their name, email, phone, the list of items they ordered as readable text, the total, and their note. Load the EmailJS library from their CDN. Keep everything else working exactly as it is now. If the email ever fails, still show the customer their confirmation and log the error to the console. Give me the full updated index.html.

prompts.title: Save orders to your database
prompts.desc: Wires Supabase in so every order is saved and never lost.
prompts.content:
Here is my current index.html:

[paste your full index.html]

I also want every order saved to my database so I never lose one. I am using Supabase. I have:
- Project URL: [paste]
- Anon public key: [paste]
- A table called orders with these columns: customer_name (text), customer_email (text), customer_phone (text), items (text), total (number), note (text). Supabase adds id and created_at on its own.

Add Supabase to my page using their JavaScript library from the CDN. When the customer taps Place order, save the order into the orders table at the same time as sending the email. Put all the items they picked into the items column as readable text. Keep the email and the on screen confirmation working. If saving ever fails, still show the confirmation and log the error to the console. Give me the full updated index.html.

prompts.title: Build your orders dashboard
prompts.desc: Creates dashboard.html: a private page where you see every order.
prompts.content:
Build me a second file called dashboard.html. This is a private page where I, the venture owner, can see all my orders.

It reads from my Supabase database. I have:
- Project URL: [paste]
- Anon public key: [paste]
- A table called orders with columns: id, created_at, customer_name, customer_phone, items, total, note.

The page should:
- Load all orders from the orders table, newest first.
- Show them in a clean list or table: when it came in, customer name, phone, what they ordered, the total, and the note.
- Show the number of orders and the total rupees at the top.
- Have a Refresh button to load the latest orders.
- Look clean and readable on a phone and a laptop.

No login for now, it is just for me. Use the Supabase JavaScript library from the CDN. Give me the full dashboard.html.

prompts.title: Fix it when something breaks
prompts.desc: Your go-to whenever a step does not work. Paste the error and your file.
prompts.content:
Something on my page is not working. Here is what is happening:

[describe exactly what you did, what you expected, and what actually happened. If there is red error text in the browser console, press F12 to open it and paste the error here.]

Here is my current file:

[paste the whole file]

Find the problem and give me back the full corrected file. Tell me in one or two plain lines what was wrong, so I understand it. Do not change anything that is already working.

output_capture.ask: Paste two links: your live order page, and your live dashboard.
output_capture.guidance: These are the Netlify addresses for your order page and your dashboard. Both should open on any phone with no login.

sections.name: Set up your tools
sections.note: You do this once. Get every account ready before you build. Take it slow here and the rest is easy.

steps.title: Set up VS Code
steps.body: This is where your files live and where you run your site on your own machine.
detail: Go to code.visualstudio.com and download VS Code for your computer. Install it like any normal app.
Open VS Code. On the left bar, click the Extensions icon, the four little squares.
Search for Live Server by Ritwick Dey and click Install. This lets you open your site in a browser with one click later.
Make one folder on your computer named after your venture. In VS Code choose File, then Open Folder, and pick it. All your files go here.

steps.title: Set up GitHub
steps.body: GitHub stores your code online and is how your site goes live. The desktop app means you never touch the command line.
detail: Go to github.com and create a free account with an email you check.
Go to desktop.github.com, download GitHub Desktop, install it, and sign in with that account.
That is all for now. You use it near the end to publish your site.

steps.title: Set up Netlify
steps.body: Netlify takes your code from GitHub and puts it on the internet, free, with its own web address.
detail: Go to netlify.com and click Sign up.
Choose Sign up with GitHub and approve it. This links the two so Netlify can see your project later.
Stop there. You connect your project at the Go live step.

steps.title: Set up EmailJS
steps.body: EmailJS emails you every time someone orders, straight from your page, with no server.
detail: Go to emailjs.com and create a free account.
Click Add New Service, choose Gmail or your email, and connect your inbox. Note the Service ID.

Click Email Templates and create a template. In the body use these fields: {{customer_name}}, {{customer_email}}, {{customer_phone}}, {{order_details}}, {{order_total}}, {{customer_note}}. Save it and note the Template ID.
Open Account and find your Public Key.
Write down three things: Public Key, Service ID, Template ID. You paste them into Prompt 2.

steps.title: Set up Supabase
steps.body: Supabase is your free database. Every order is saved here, and you get a built in table view as a bonus.
detail: Go to supabase.com, sign up free, and click New project. Give it your venture name and a password you save. Wait about a minute.
On the left open Table Editor and click New table. Name it exactly orders. Turn off Enable Row Level Security for now to keep things simple.
Add these columns, besides the id and created_at it makes for you: customer_name (text), customer_email (text), customer_phone (text), items (text), total (numeric), note (text). Save the table.
On the left open Project Settings, then API. Copy two things: the Project URL and the anon public key. You paste them into Prompts 3 and 4.

sections.name: Build the order app
sections.note: Now you build, one layer at a time, with Gemini. Each prompt adds one piece.

steps.title: Run Prompt 1, build your order page
steps.body: Open Gemini and run Prompt 1. Fill the brackets with your real venture and offerings. Gemini gives you a full index.html.
detail: Open the prompts page on your laptop and copy Prompt 1.
Paste it into Gemini. Replace every part in brackets with your own venture, your offerings, their prices, and details.
Gemini writes a full index.html. Read what it made. If something is off, tell Gemini in plain words and it will fix it.

steps.title: Put it in VS Code
steps.body: Get Gemini's code into a real file on your computer.
detail: In VS Code choose File, then New File. Save it as index.html inside your venture folder.
Copy all of Gemini's code, paste it into that file, and save. Ctrl S on Windows, Cmd S on Mac.

steps.title: Run Prompt 2, add the email
steps.body: Now make it email you on every order, using your EmailJS keys.
detail: Copy Prompt 2 from the prompts page.
Paste it into the same Gemini chat. Paste your full index.html where it asks, and paste your three EmailJS values.
Gemini returns the updated index.html. Copy it back into your VS Code file, replacing what was there, and save.

steps.title: Run Prompt 3, save to the database
steps.body: Now make every order save to Supabase as well.
detail: Copy Prompt 3 from the prompts page.
Paste it into the same Gemini chat. Paste your current index.html and your Supabase Project URL and anon key.
Gemini returns the updated index.html. Copy it back into your VS Code file, replacing what was there, and save.

sections.name: Build the dashboard
sections.note: A private page, just for you, to see your orders.

steps.title: Run Prompt 4, build your dashboard
steps.body: Gemini builds a second page that reads your orders from Supabase.
detail: Copy Prompt 4 from the prompts page.
Paste it into Gemini with your Supabase Project URL and anon key.
Gemini writes a full dashboard.html.

steps.title: Put it in VS Code
steps.body: Save the dashboard next to your order page.
detail: In VS Code choose File, then New File. Save it as dashboard.html inside the same venture folder.
Paste Gemini's dashboard code in and save.

sections.name: Test it on your own machine
sections.note: Find the problems here, on your laptop, before anyone else sees them.

steps.title: Open it with Live Server
steps.body: Run your site locally with one click.
detail: In VS Code right click index.html and choose Open with Live Server. Your order page opens in your browser.
This is your site running on your own machine. Nobody else can see it yet.

steps.title: Place a test order
steps.body: Use your own page the way a customer would.
detail: Pick an offering, set a quantity, fill in your own name and phone, and tap Place order.
You should see the confirmation on screen.

steps.title: Check all three landed
steps.body: One order should show up in three places.
detail: Your email: an order email should arrive in the inbox you connected to EmailJS. Check spam too.
Your database: open Supabase, Table Editor, the orders table. Your test order should be a new row.
Your dashboard: open dashboard.html with Live Server. Your order should appear in the list.

steps.title: Run Prompt 5 if anything is off
steps.body: Something not working? This is your fix-it prompt.
detail: If the page looks wrong, the email did not arrive, or the order did not save, do not panic.
Copy Prompt 5 from the prompts page. Tell Gemini exactly what you did and what happened, paste any red error text from the browser console (press F12), and paste your file.
Gemini returns a corrected file. Replace your VS Code file, save, and test again. Repeat until all three land.

sections.name: Put it live
sections.note: Now the whole world can use it.

steps.title: Publish with GitHub Desktop
steps.body: Send your folder to GitHub in a few clicks.
detail: Open GitHub Desktop. Choose File, then Add Local Repository, and pick your venture folder. If it asks, click to create a repository, then Create.
Your files show up as changes. At the bottom left type a short summary like first version and click Commit to main.
Click Publish repository at the top. Keep it private if you like. Your code is now on GitHub.

steps.title: Connect Netlify and go live
steps.body: Netlify turns your GitHub project into a live website.
detail: In Netlify click Add new site, then Import an existing project, then GitHub. Pick your venture repository.
Leave the build settings empty, this is a plain site, and click Deploy.
In under a minute Netlify gives you a live web address like yourname.netlify.app. That is your order page, live. Your dashboard is the same address with /dashboard.html on the end.
From now on, whenever you commit and push in GitHub Desktop, Netlify updates your live site on its own.

steps.title: Test the real thing on your phone
steps.body: Open the live site on your phone, as a customer.
detail: Open your netlify.app address on your phone and place a real order.
Ask a friend to open it on their phone and order too. This is a real customer using your real product.

steps.title: Confirm it all works live
steps.body: The same three checks, now in the real world.
detail: The order email arrived in your inbox.
The order is a new row in your Supabase orders table.
The order shows on your live dashboard at /dashboard.html.

steps.title: Declare success
steps.body: You built and launched a real working product, powered by AI.
detail: Your order page is live and takes real orders.
You get an email for every order, every order is saved, and you have a dashboard to see them all.
Save two links for your portfolio: your live order page, and your live dashboard. Paste them into the cohort form.

sessions.title: Plan the website + marketing collaterals
sessions.set_the_stage:
You ship the website tomorrow morning. Tonight, you plan it.

A solopreneur without a website is like a shopkeeper without a shopfront. Your website is a conversation with a stranger who has never heard of you. A conversation that has to answer three questions in thirty seconds. What is this? Is this for me? What do I do next?

The biggest reason websites take twice as long as they should is that founders try to write copy live, in the builder, staring at a blank page. We are not doing that. Tonight you write all your copy in a document. Tomorrow you just drop it in.

Three pages of copy: landing, about, and how it works. By the end of this session you walk out with your website copy written and ready for the morning.

No builder tonight. No design. Just writing.

prompts.title: Write your landing page copy
prompts.desc: The words a stranger reads first: what this is, who it is for, and what to do next.
prompts.content:
I am writing the copy for the landing page of my small venture's website. It is not an AI product, I just want clear words that make a stranger understand and act.

My venture:
- What I sell: [one line]
- Who it is for: [one line, my customer]
- The problem I solve for them: [one line]
- What makes me different or trustworthy: [one or two lines]
- The one action I want a visitor to take: [for example place an order, message me, sign up]

Write landing page copy that answers three things fast: what is this, is it for me, what do I do next. Give me:
- A headline under 10 words.
- A one line subheadline.
- Three short sections, each with a small heading and two or three lines.
- The wording for one clear button.
- A short closing line.

Plain, warm, simple English. Short sentences. No buzzwords, no jargon. Indian context is fine. Give it as plain text I can paste into a document.

prompts.title: Write your about page copy
prompts.desc: The human story that turns a visitor into someone who trusts you.
prompts.content:
Write the About page copy for my venture's website. This page builds trust by telling the human story.

My venture:
- What I sell: [one line]
- Who I am: [your name, one line about you]
- Why I started this: [one or two lines, the real reason]
- Who I serve and what I promise them: [one or two lines]

Write a short About page: a warm opening line, one short paragraph on why this exists, one short paragraph on who I am, and a closing line that invites the next step. Honest and human, not corporate. Plain English, short sentences. Give it as plain text I can paste into a document.

prompts.title: Write your how-it-works page copy
prompts.desc: The steps that remove doubt and make ordering feel easy.
prompts.content:
Write the How it works page copy for my venture's website. This page removes doubt by showing the steps clearly.

My venture:
- What I sell: [one line]
- How a customer gets it start to finish: [list the real steps, for example they browse, they order, I confirm, I deliver]
- Anything they should know: [timing, area served, payment method, etc.]

Write a simple How it works page: a one line intro, then three or four numbered steps each with a short heading and one or two lines, then a short reassurance line and the wording for a button to start. Plain English, no jargon. Give it as plain text I can paste into a document.

sections.name: Write your website copy
sections.note: Tonight you only write. Tomorrow you build. Writing first means no blank page in the morning.

steps.title: Run Prompt 1, landing page copy
steps.body: Claude writes the words a stranger reads first.
detail: Open the build guide on your laptop and copy Prompt 1.
Paste it into Claude and fill the brackets with your venture.
Read what it wrote. If a line feels off, tell Claude and it will redo it.

steps.title: Run Prompt 2, about page copy
steps.body: Claude writes your human story.
detail: Copy Prompt 2 and paste it into Claude with your details.
Make sure it sounds like you, not a brochure.

steps.title: Run Prompt 3, how it works copy
steps.body: Claude writes the simple steps a customer follows.
detail: Copy Prompt 3 and paste it into Claude with your real steps.
Check the steps match how you actually work.

steps.title: Save all three in one document
steps.body: Keep your copy in one place for the morning.
detail: Open a Google Doc or a Word file. Title it your venture website copy.
Paste all three pages in, each under a clear heading: Landing, About, How it works.
Save it where you can find it tomorrow.

steps.title: Read it out loud, once
steps.body: Your ear catches what your eye misses.
detail: Read all three pages out loud.
Fix any line that is hard to say or sounds stiff.

steps.title: Show one peer in the room
steps.body: A fresh reader finds the confusing bits fast.
detail: Hand your laptop to one person near you.
Watch where they slow down or look puzzled. Fix those lines.

sessions.title: Launch the website
sessions.set_the_stage:
Three hours. You walked in with three pages of copy written and ready. You walk out with a live three-page website at a real URL: a landing page, an about page, and a how-it-works page, all linked together, all looking like they belong, with a working button that takes a visitor to your app.

This is Launch. The shopfront opens. The audio, the founder video, and the brochure all come next, in the Brand session after lunch. Right now the priority is the website itself: three pages, your copy in place, mobile ready, app linked.

You build it the same way you built the app: the AI writes the HTML, you paste it into VS Code, and you put it live with the same GitHub and Netlify you set up yesterday. The facilitator walks the room. If you finish early, do not race ahead to Brand. Polish what you have, open it on a friend's phone, read it aloud once more.

prompts.title: Build your landing page
prompts.desc: Turns your landing copy into a real index.html, with an Order button to your app.
prompts.content:
I am building the landing page for my venture's website. I want one HTML file I can host for free, with no backend.

Here is my landing page copy:

[paste your landing page copy from last night]

My venture name is [name]. My order app is already live at [paste your app URL].

Build me one file called index.html that:
- Lays out my copy cleanly: headline, subheadline, the sections, and the closing line.
- Has a clear Order now button that links to my order app URL and opens it.
- Has a simple top menu linking to About and How it works. Name those links about.html and how-it-works.html. I will build those pages next.
- Looks clean and modern, reads well on a phone, simple colours, my venture name at the top.
- Leaves a little space just under the headline where I will add a short audio clip later.

Give me the full index.html.

prompts.title: Build your about and how-it-works pages
prompts.desc: Builds the other two pages to match the landing page, all linked together.
prompts.content:
Here is my landing page so the new pages match its look:

[paste your index.html]

Using the same colours, fonts, header, and menu, build me two more pages.

1. about.html, using this copy:
[paste your about page copy]

2. how-it-works.html, using this copy:
[paste your how it works page copy]

Both pages must have the same top menu linking Home (index.html), About (about.html), and How it works (how-it-works.html), so all three link to each other. Keep the Order now button on every page, linking to my app. Give me both files in full.

prompts.title: Fix it when something breaks
prompts.desc: Your go-to whenever a page looks wrong or a link does not work.
prompts.content:
Something on my page is not working. Here is what is happening:

[describe what you did, what you expected, and what actually happened. If there is red error text in the browser console, press F12 and paste it here.]

Here is my current file:

[paste the whole file]

Find the problem and give me back the full corrected file. Tell me in one or two plain lines what was wrong. Do not change anything that is already working.

output_capture.ask: Paste the live URL of your website.
output_capture.guidance: This is the Netlify address for your website. Check all three pages load on a phone and the Order now button opens your app. Audio, video, and brochure come next, in the Brand session.

sections.name: Use yesterday's tools
sections.note: You already set these up for the app. No new accounts today.

steps.title: You have everything you need
steps.body: VS Code, GitHub Desktop, and Netlify are ready from yesterday.
detail: Make a new folder on your computer for your website, separate from your app folder.
In VS Code, File then Open Folder, and pick it.

Leave the build settings empty and click Deploy.

Click through all three pages and the Order now button.

Your Order now button takes visitors to your working app.

sessions.title: Brand with audio, video, and brochure
sessions.set_the_stage:
Three hours. The website is live. Now you give it a voice, a face, and something a visitor can carry away with them.

This is Brand. Three pieces. Sixty seconds of audio in your voice, generated from your script, embedded on the landing page right after the headline. Ninety seconds of founder video, screen-recorded with your voice over it, posted as a native video on LinkedIn. One one-page brochure, generated as a PDF, linked from your website for visitors who want to share it with a parent or relative.

All three exist for the same reason: trust. A website is a stranger reading words. Audio, video, and brochure turn that stranger into someone who has met you.

The facilitator walks the room continuously. Lunch is the first 30 minutes of this block if you have not eaten yet , take it now. Be done by 15:00. Interim demo at 15:00. Hard deadline.

prompts.title: Write your 60-second audio script
prompts.desc: A short, warm script in your voice, to play on your landing page.
prompts.content:
Write me a 60-second audio script for my venture, in my voice, to play on my website's landing page.

My venture:
- What I sell: [one line]
- Who it is for: [one line]
- The problem I solve: [one line]
- What I want the listener to do after: [one line]

Write it to be spoken in about 60 seconds, so roughly 140 to 150 words. Warm, friendly, first person, like I am talking to one person. Short sentences, no jargon. Start with a hook, say what I offer and why it helps, end with a gentle invitation to act. Give me just the script text.

prompts.title: Add the audio to your landing page
prompts.desc: Adds a small audio player to your landing page, just under the headline.
prompts.content:
Here is my landing page:

[paste your index.html]

I have an audio file called voice.mp3 that I will put in the same folder. Add a clean, simple audio player to my landing page, just below the headline, with a small label like: Hear it from me in 60 seconds. Keep everything else exactly as it is. Give me the full updated index.html.

prompts.title: Write your 90-second founder video script
prompts.desc: What you say while screen-recording a walk through your site and app.
prompts.content:
Write me a 90-second founder video script. I will screen-record myself walking through my website and app while I talk.

My venture:
- What I sell: [one line]
- Who I am: [name, one line]
- Why I built this: [one line]
- What I will show on screen: my website and my order app

Write it as what I say out loud, about 90 seconds, roughly 200 to 220 words, with short notes in brackets for what to show on screen at each part. First person, warm, confident, plain English. Start by introducing myself and the problem, walk through the site and app, end by inviting people to try it or share it. Give me just the script with the on-screen notes.

prompts.title: Write your LinkedIn caption
prompts.desc: The post that goes out with your founder video.
prompts.content:
Write a short LinkedIn caption to post with my 90-second founder video.

My venture: [one line on what I sell and who it is for]
What I built: a working website and app, on my own, powered by AI
The link I want people to visit: [your website URL]

Write a warm, genuine caption, about 4 to 6 short lines, first person. Open with a hook, say what I built and why, invite people to watch and try it, end with the link. A few relevant hashtags at the end. No corporate tone, no exaggeration. Give me just the caption.

prompts.title: Write your one-page brochure copy
prompts.desc: Copy in clean blocks to drop into a Canva template.
prompts.content:
Write the copy for a one-page brochure for my venture. I will put it into a Canva template and export it as a PDF.

My venture:
- What I sell: [one line]
- Who it is for: [one line]
- The problem I solve: [one line]
- My offerings, briefly: [list a few]
- How to order or reach me: [your website URL, phone, area served]

Write brochure copy in clear blocks I can drop into a template: a headline, a one line tagline, a short who-this-is-for line, three or four short benefit points, a brief how-to-order line, and my contact details. Plain English, short, scannable. Label each block clearly.

output_capture.ask: Paste your LinkedIn founder video post URL.
output_capture.guidance: Audio and brochure live on your website (already captured in the Launch session). The founder video is the one new public URL from Brand , pinned to your LinkedIn profile and linked from your website.

sections.name: Set up your tools

steps.title: Set up ElevenLabs

Open the text to speech tool. You will paste your script here in the audio step.

steps.body: Records your screen and your voice at the same time.
detail: Go to loom.com, create a free account, and install the Loom app or browser extension.
Allow it to use your microphone and screen.
Do a 5 second test recording so you know Start and Stop.

steps.title: Set up Canva

detail: Go to canva.com and create a free account.
You will search for a flyer or one-page template in the brochure step.

sections.name: Audio, 60 seconds in your voice

Pick a voice you like.
Generate, listen, and when happy download the file. Rename it voice.mp3.

detail: Copy Prompt 2 and paste your index.html into ChatGPT.
It returns the updated index.html. Paste it back into VS Code and save.
In GitHub Desktop, commit and push. Netlify updates your live site. Refresh and hear it play.

sections.name: Founder video, 90 seconds
sections.note: Script, record, caption, post.

steps.title: Run Prompt 3, write your video script

steps.title: Record it with Loom

Stop. Loom gives you a shareable link. Copy it.

Adjust the wording to sound like you.

You now have a headline, tagline, benefits, and contact details ready.

sections.note: All three, working, in the real world.
