Your audio and brochure live on your website.
Paste your LinkedIn video URL into the cohort form.

sessions.title: Interim Demo Day
sessions.set_the_stage:
Stop building. Stop polishing. Stand up.

For the last day and a half you have been heads-down. Now the room shifts. Every person in this cohort has ninety seconds to show what they made.

This is not the real Demo Day. The real Demo Day is in four weeks, with the full portfolio, recorded, shareable, in front of guests. This is the interim demo. Peer to peer, no slides, no rehearsal. You stand up, open your site on the screen, walk the room through the user's journey in ninety seconds, and sit down. The room claps. The next person stands up.

This hour is the most important hour of the weekend. Not because of the demos themselves, but because of what watching the whole room demo does to your own bar. By the time the room finishes, you will have ideas for what to add over the next four weeks. Write them down as you watch.

build.title: The ninety-second demo format
build.instructions: Open your Carrd site on the projector. Three beats. Beat 1: who this is for and what they were doing before. Beat 2: open the AI app and run one real input, the room watches the output appear. Beat 3: one thing you learned building this that surprised you. Sit down. The room claps.

build.title: Watch and write
build.instructions: While others demo, keep a doc open. Whenever you see an idea you want to steal, a section structure, a phrasing, a moment in the AI output, write it down. You will look at this list during the four-week stretch.

build.title: Three demos get the room's vote
build.instructions: At the end of the hour, the room votes for three demos that stood out. One for clearest problem, one for tightest AI output, one for sharpest delivery. Not winners. Just signal.

test_output.title: The demo test
test_output.description: Did every person in the cohort demo in under ninety seconds? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The notebook test
test_output.description: Did you write down at least five ideas from other people's demos to apply to your own build over the next four weeks? If yes, pass.

sessions.title: The four-week plan
sessions.set_the_stage:
You walked in two days ago with an idea on a card. You leave with a live AI app, a three-page website, an audio piece, a founder video, and a brochure. Five real things. All live. All yours.

The next four weeks are where the harder builds happen. Chatbot. Agent. Then Demo Day. You will do most of this on your own, with two forms of support. A weekly ninety-minute office hours session, this room or on Meet, same time, every week. And a WhatsApp group for between-session unblocks.

Forty-five minutes to hand over the materials, walk through the four-week shape, set the norms for how we support you, and lock the Demo Day date on every calendar.

questions.title: What hour of the week is yours?
questions.body: Pick a specific hour, every week, that is your Just Build hour. Not 'whenever I find time'. A specific hour, that fits around your classes and your assignments. Put it in your phone calendar before you leave the room. The students who finish the four weeks are the ones who treated this hour as non-negotiable.

questions.title: Who is your accountability partner?
questions.body: Pair up with one person in the room. Once a week, on your Just Build hour, you text each other one update. What you finished, what you are stuck on. Two-sentence accountability. Pick now. Exchange numbers.

build.title: Hand-out, the chatbot PDF
build.instructions: Every student gets the chatbot build PDF on their email and on WhatsApp. Detailed, step-by-step, screenshots for every screen. The expectation is you complete the chatbot build in Week 1 and bring questions to the Week 1 office hours.

build.title: Hand-out, the agent PDF
build.instructions: Every student gets the agent build PDF on their email and on WhatsApp. This is the harder build. Two weeks. Bring questions to the Week 2 and Week 3 office hours.

build.title: Office hours, same room, every Saturday
build.instructions: This room (or on Meet), this time, every Saturday, for the next four weeks. Ninety minutes. Facilitator walks through common stuck points proactively. The rest is live Q&A. Treat it as a meeting, not a class. You bring your screen, your question, your unblock.

build.title: WhatsApp group, norms
build.instructions: Group created tonight. Post screenshots, not text descriptions. 'It is not working' with no screenshot gets ignored. Screenshot plus what you tried plus the error message gets a reply within a few hours. Office hours are for stuck-for-thirty-minutes problems. WhatsApp is for quick unblocks.

build.title: Demo Day date and shape
build.instructions: Demo Day is locked. Date is on the calendar invite the facilitator just shared. Guests are invited. Alumni, recruiters, family if you want. Each of you gets three minutes plus one minute Q&A. Hard cutoff. Demos are recorded. The recording becomes part of your portfolio.

test_output.title: The calendar test
test_output.description: Is your weekly Just Build hour in your calendar? Are the four office hours dates in your calendar? Is Demo Day in your calendar? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The partner test
test_output.description: Do you know one person's name and number in this room, who you have agreed to check in with weekly? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The materials test
test_output.description: Do you have the chatbot PDF, the agent PDF, and the WhatsApp invite link on your phone before you leave this room? If yes, pass.

sessions.title: Strengthen the Launch with a chatbot
sessions.set_the_stage:
Welcome back. Week 1.

The Launch is live. The website is up, the audio is embedded, the video is on LinkedIn, the brochure is downloadable. What is missing is the moment when a visitor has a question and you are not there to answer. The chatbot is what fills that gap. It strengthens the Launch by giving every visitor someone to talk to, every hour of every day.

By now everyone in this room has worked through the chatbot steps in the build guide. Some of you have a chatbot live on your website. Some of you are stuck. Some of you have not started. All three are normal at this point.

Ninety minutes. We open with a quick status check, then walk through the four places students most commonly get stuck on the chatbot build, then open the floor for live Q&A. The last fifteen minutes we close with a roster check. Every person in the room confirms, out loud, whether their chatbot is live or what specifically they need to land it by next Saturday.
agenda: First 10 min, status check. Go around the room. Each person in one sentence: chatbot live, in progress, not started, or specifically stuck
Next 20 min, walkthrough of the four common stuck points on the chatbot build
Next 45 min, live Q&A. Screens up. Bring your specific question
Last 15 min, roster check. Every person says: live, or specifically what is left

stuck_points.title: The system prompt is too short
stuck_points.resolution: A chatbot system prompt of three sentences produces a chatbot that says useful things three times before going generic. The PDF has the long-form template. Use it. If yours is short, expand it before doing anything else.

stuck_points.title: The chatbot has no scope
stuck_points.resolution: Without an explicit 'do not answer questions outside X' instruction, the chatbot answers anything and feels like generic ChatGPT. Add the scope sentence at the top of the system prompt.

stuck_points.title: The embed code does not show up on Carrd
stuck_points.resolution: Carrd's free tier blocks some script tags. Use the iframe embed option instead, not the script embed. The PDF has the exact iframe HTML.

stuck_points.title: The chatbot never points to the AI app
stuck_points.resolution: The chatbot exists to convert visitors into AI app users. Every long answer should end with a soft invitation back to the app. Add that instruction to the system prompt explicitly.

prompts.title: Write your chatbot's FAQ
prompts.desc: The questions customers ask and the answers your bot will give. This is the bot's brain.
prompts.content:
I want to add a small help chatbot to my venture's website. It answers the common questions visitors ask, using answers I write. It is not a live AI, it uses my own answers.

My venture:
- What I sell: [one line]
- Who it is for: [one line]
- How customers order: [one line]
- Delivery or service area: [one line]
- Timings or availability: [one line]
- Prices, roughly: [a line]

Help me build the FAQ for my chatbot. Give me the 8 to 12 questions a real customer is most likely to ask, and write a short, friendly answer for each in my voice, one or two lines each. Cover what I offer, prices, how to order, area, and timings. Add one answer for when someone asks something we have not covered, telling them to message me on WhatsApp at [your number] or use the Order button. Give it to me as a clean list of question and answer pairs.

prompts.title: Build your chatbot widget
prompts.desc: Turns your FAQ into a chat bubble you can paste onto every page of your site.
prompts.content:
Build me a small chat help widget for my website, as one self-contained block of HTML, CSS, and JavaScript I can paste into my pages. No backend, no API, no external services. It uses only the FAQ I give you below.

My venture name is [name].

Here is my FAQ:
[paste the question and answer pairs from the last step]

The widget should:
- Show a small chat bubble in the bottom right corner of the page.
- Open a chat panel when tapped, with a friendly greeting in my venture's name.
- Show my common questions as tappable buttons, so a visitor can pick one and see the answer.
- Also let them type. Match what they type to the closest FAQ question and show that answer.
- When nothing matches, show my fallback answer (message on WhatsApp or use the Order button).
- Look clean and simple, and work well on a phone.

Give me the whole thing as one block I can paste right before the closing body tag, and tell me clearly where to paste it.

prompts.title: Fix it when something breaks
prompts.desc: Your go-to if the bubble does not show or an answer is wrong.
prompts.content:
Something with my chatbot is not working. Here is what is happening:

[describe what you did, what you expected, and what happened. If there is red error text in the browser console, press F12 and paste it here.]

Here is my current file:

[paste the whole file, or the widget block]

Find the problem and give me back the full corrected version. Tell me in one or two plain lines what was wrong. Do not change anything that is already working.

sessions.live_qa_note: Open the floor for 40 to 45 minutes. Screens up. Specific questions only. If someone has a general 'I am stuck' question, ask them to share their screen and walk through what they tried.
sessions.homework_check: Every person, around the room, in one sentence: chatbot live by next Saturday, or specifically what is left.

output_capture.ask: Paste your website URL again, now with the chatbot on it.
output_capture.guidance: The chatbot lives on your website as a small chat bubble. Open the URL on your phone and check the bubble appears and answers on every page.

sections.name: Write your chatbot's answers
sections.note: Your chatbot is only as good as the answers you give it. Write these first. Building today is on Mistral.

steps.title: Run Prompt 1, write your FAQ
steps.body: Mistral helps you list the real questions and write friendly answers.
detail: Open the build guide on your laptop and copy Prompt 1.
Paste it into Mistral and fill in your venture details.
Read the answers. Fix anything wrong or that does not sound like you. These answers are your chatbot's brain.

steps.title: Save your FAQ
steps.body: Keep the question and answer pairs ready for the next step.
detail: Copy the final list of questions and answers somewhere you can paste it again.

sections.name: Build the widget
sections.note: Mistral turns your FAQ into a chat bubble for your site.

steps.title: Run Prompt 2, build your chatbot
steps.body: Mistral gives you one block of code to paste onto your site.
detail: Copy Prompt 2. Paste it into Mistral with your venture name and your FAQ.
Mistral returns one block of code and tells you where to paste it.

steps.title: Add it to all your pages
steps.body: The chatbot should appear on every page of your site.
detail: Open your website folder in VS Code.
Paste the widget block right before the </body> tag on index.html, about.html, and how-it-works.html. The same block on all three.
Save each file.

sections.name: Test it on your machine
sections.note: Try the bot before anyone else does.

steps.title: Open it with Live Server
steps.body: Run your site and look for the bubble.
detail: Right click index.html and choose Open with Live Server.
You should see the chat bubble in the corner.

steps.title: Try the questions
steps.body: Use it like a curious visitor.
detail: Open the chat. Tap a few question buttons and check the answers.
Type a question in your own words and see if it finds the right answer.
Type something random and check you get the friendly fallback.
Check the bubble shows on all three pages.

steps.title: Run Prompt 3 if anything is off
steps.body: Bubble missing or an answer wrong? This is your fix-it prompt.
detail: Copy Prompt 3. Tell Mistral what happened and paste your file.
Paste the fix back into VS Code, save, and test again.

sections.name: Put it live
sections.note: Push the change and let Netlify update your site.

steps.title: Publish your update
steps.body: Commit and push your changed pages.
detail: In GitHub Desktop you will see your changed pages. Type a summary like added chatbot, click Commit to main, then Push.
Netlify updates your live site in under a minute.

steps.title: Test it live on your phone
steps.body: See the bot the way a visitor will.
detail: Open your live website on your phone.
Tap the chat bubble and ask a couple of questions.

steps.title: Your chatbot is live
steps.body: Every visitor now has someone to talk to, any hour.
detail: Your chatbot answers the common questions on every page.
It points people to order or message you when it does not know.
Your website URL is unchanged, the chatbot is now on it. Paste your website URL into the cohort form.

sessions.title: Office hours, agent, week 1
sessions.set_the_stage:
Week 2. The agent build is harder than the chatbot build. That is by design. You have two weeks to ship it, not one.

This week is the plumbing. By the end you have a Make.com account, your orders flowing from Supabase into Make, and a confirmation email going out to the customer on every order, even if the wording is still rough.

The agent is the assistant that handles what happens after an order. A customer orders on your app, it lands in your Supabase, and the agent, with no one watching, sends them a warm confirmation. Next week we make it sound like you and add a daily summary for yourself.

We open with a status check, walk the common stuck points for this week's plumbing, then open the floor. The last fifteen minutes, a roster check: confirmation sending, or specifically what is left.
agenda: First 10 min, status check. Each person: orders reaching Make, in progress, not started, or specifically stuck
Next 20 min, walkthrough of the common stuck points wiring Supabase to Make
Next 45 min, live Q&A. Screens up, Make scenarios open
Last 15 min, roster check. Every person says: confirmation sending, or specifically what is left

stuck_points.title: Supabase is not sending new orders to Make
stuck_points.resolution: Check the database webhook in Supabase points to the exact Make webhook URL and is set for the Insert event on the orders table. Then place one test order so Make receives a sample.

stuck_points.title: Make runs but does not see the order fields
stuck_points.resolution: In the webhook module, click to redetermine the data structure, then place one more test order. The fields, including the customer email, will appear so you can map them.

stuck_points.title: The free tier is hitting the operations limit
stuck_points.resolution: Free is 1,000 operations a month, plenty for testing. Turn the scenario off while you are not actively testing and on when you demo. Two scenarios is the free ceiling, which is exactly what this build needs.

stuck_points.title: The confirmation email is not arriving
stuck_points.resolution: Check the email module is connected to your inbox and the To field is mapped to the customer's email from the order, not a fixed address. Check spam. On free, allow up to about 15 minutes.

prompts.title: Write your order confirmation email
prompts.desc: The warm note the customer gets the moment they order. The agent sends it for you.
prompts.content:
I want to send a customer a warm confirmation email the moment they place an order on my venture's website. An automation will send it, so write the email with placeholders I can fill from the order.

My venture:
- What I sell: [one line]
- My name and how I sign off: [your name]
- What happens after they order: [one line, for example I confirm and deliver by 8pm]

Write a short, warm confirmation email in my voice. Use {{name}} for the customer's name and {{items}} for what they ordered. Include: a friendly thank you using their name, a line confirming what they ordered, a line on what happens next, and a warm sign off from me. Keep it human and specific, not a robotic no-reply. Give me a subject line and the body, as plain text.

sessions.live_qa_note: Open the floor for 40 to 45 minutes. Screens up, Make.com scenarios visible. Most agent debugging happens by looking at the scenario diagram together.
sessions.homework_check: Every person, around the room, in one sentence: trigger-to-email working by next Saturday, or specifically what is left.

sections.name: Set up Make and check your orders
sections.note: The agent lives in Make.com and reads the orders already in your Supabase. Writing today is on Claude.

steps.title: Create a free Make account
steps.body: This is where your automation lives.
detail: Go to make.com and sign up for the free plan. No card needed.
The free plan gives you two scenarios and 1,000 operations a month, which is exactly what this build needs.

steps.title: Check your order app collects email
steps.body: The agent emails the customer, so your orders need the customer's email.
detail: Open your order app. The form should ask for the customer's email, alongside name and phone.
Open Supabase, your orders table. It should have a customer_email column.
If either is missing, ask your build AI to add an email field to the form and save it to a customer_email column, then redeploy. Most of you already collect it.

sections.name: Wire your orders to the agent
sections.note: Supabase will tell Make every time a new order comes in.

steps.title: Start Scenario 1 with a webhook
steps.body: Create the entry point the order will arrive at.
detail: In Make, create a new scenario. Add the Webhooks module, then Custom webhook. Create one and copy the URL it gives you.

steps.title: Send new orders from Supabase to Make
steps.body: Tell Supabase to ping Make whenever an order is added.
detail: In Supabase, open Database, then Webhooks, and create a new webhook on the orders table for the Insert event.
Set the URL to the Make webhook URL you copied, and save.
If you cannot find database webhooks, ask your AI how to send a Supabase insert to a webhook URL and follow the steps.

steps.title: Test the connection
steps.body: Make a real order and watch it arrive in Make.
detail: Place a test order on your app.
In Make, run the scenario once and confirm the order's details, name, email, and items, show up. If Make does not see the fields, redetermine the data structure and order again.

sections.name: Send the customer a confirmation
sections.note: Now the agent replies to every order, automatically.

steps.title: Run Prompt 1, write your confirmation email
steps.body: Claude writes a warm confirmation with placeholders.
detail: Open the build guide, copy Prompt 1, and paste it into Claude with your venture details.
You get a subject line and body with {{name}} and {{items}} placeholders.

steps.title: Add the email step in Make
steps.body: Make sends your email to the customer on every order.
detail: In your scenario, add an Email module, Send an email, and connect a free email such as your Gmail.
In the To field, map the customer's email from the order. Put in your subject and body, and map {{name}} and {{items}} to the order's fields.
Turn the scenario on.

steps.title: Test it end to end
steps.body: One real order should produce one real email.
detail: Place a test order using your own email as the customer.
Within about 15 minutes you should get the confirmation. Check spam too.
Week 1 is done when an order reliably produces a confirmation, even if the wording is still rough.

sessions.title: Office hours, agent, week 2
sessions.set_the_stage:
Week 3. The agent should be live by next Saturday.

The plumbing is done: an order comes in and a confirmation goes out. This week is two things. First, voice: make that confirmation sound like the same person who wrote your website and narrated your video, not a no-reply from an internet company. Second, the daily digest: a once-a-day summary email to yourself, read straight from your Supabase orders.

The email module already works. The question is whether what it sends sounds like you, and whether you get a clean view of your day without scrolling. We walk this week's stuck points, then open the floor. By next Saturday: a confirmation in your voice, plus a daily digest, both live.
agenda: First 10 min, status check. Each person: confirmation live and improving, digest in progress, or specifically stuck
Next 20 min, walkthrough of this week's stuck points, voice and the digest
Next 45 min, live Q&A. Bring your confirmation email, the room critiques the voice
Last 15 min, roster check. Every person says: agent live, or specifically what is left for Demo Day

stuck_points.title: The confirmation reads like an auto-notification
stuck_points.resolution: Pull your website copy and your video script. The email must sound like the same person. Rewrite the body so it leads with what they actually ordered, then your voice. Prompt 1 this week does exactly this.

stuck_points.title: The digest cannot read today's orders from Supabase
stuck_points.resolution: In the HTTP module you read the orders table filtered to today's date. Ask your AI for the exact Supabase read for today's rows, and put your Project URL and anon key in the request. Test by running the scenario once.

stuck_points.title: The agent breaks on a weird order
stuck_points.resolution: Add a filter so the confirmation only sends when the customer email and items are both present. Empty or junk orders are skipped, not sent.

stuck_points.title: Both scenarios will not stay on
stuck_points.resolution: Free allows exactly two active scenarios, which is what you have: the confirmation and the digest. If a third is on from testing, turn it off, and keep only these two active.

prompts.title: Polish your confirmation to sound like you
prompts.desc: Rewrites the confirmation so it matches your website and video voice.
prompts.content:
Here is my current order confirmation email:

[paste your current confirmation email]

Here is my website landing copy and my video script, so the email matches my voice:

[paste your landing copy and your video script]

Rewrite the confirmation so it sounds like the same person who wrote those, warm and specific, not like an automated notification. Keep the {{name}} and {{items}} placeholders. Lead with something that references what they actually ordered. Keep it short. Give me the subject line and body.

prompts.title: Write your daily order digest
prompts.desc: The once-a-day summary email you get, read from your Supabase orders.
prompts.content:
Write me a short daily summary email that I, the venture owner, get each evening listing the day's orders.

My venture: [one line]

The automation will give you a list of today's orders, each with a name, items, and total. Write an email that opens with the count and total for the day, for example 12 orders, 4,800 rupees, then lists each order on its own line as name, items, total, and ends with a short encouraging sign off to myself. Use {{count}}, {{total}}, and {{orders_list}} as placeholders. Keep it clean and skimmable. Give me a subject line and body.

sessions.live_qa_note: Less debugging this week, more critique. Have students paste sample agent emails into the projector. The room reads them, names what sounds off, suggests fixes.
sessions.homework_check: Every person, around the room: agent fully live for Demo Day, or specifically what is left. Anyone with more than one specific issue gets a one-to-one slot with the facilitator before Demo Day.

output_capture.ask: Paste your app URL. Your agent now runs behind it.
output_capture.guidance: There is no separate URL for the agent, it works behind your app and Supabase. Paste your app URL. On Demo Day, place a live order to show the confirmation arrive, and show your daily digest.

sections.name: Make the confirmation sound like you
sections.note: The plumbing works. Now the voice. Writing is on Claude.

steps.title: Run Prompt 1, polish your confirmation
steps.body: Claude rewrites the email to match your website and video voice.
detail: Copy Prompt 1 and paste it into Claude with your current email, your landing copy, and your video script.
You get a warmer version that references what they ordered.

steps.title: Update the email in Make
steps.body: Swap in the better wording.
detail: Open Scenario 1 in Make. Replace the subject and body with your polished version, keeping the mapped {{name}} and {{items}}.
Place a test order and read the email as if you were the customer. Adjust until it sounds like you.

steps.title: Handle the odd order
steps.body: Make sure a weird order does not break it.
detail: An order with no email or no items should be skipped, not sent broken.
In Make, add a filter so the email only sends when the customer email and items are present. Ask in office hours if you are unsure.

sections.name: Add the daily digest
sections.note: A once-a-day summary to you, read from your Supabase orders.

steps.title: Run Prompt 2, write your digest
steps.body: Claude writes your evening summary email.
detail: Copy Prompt 2 and paste it into Claude with your venture.
You get a clean summary format with {{count}}, {{total}}, and {{orders_list}} placeholders.

steps.title: Build Scenario 2 in Make
steps.body: A second scenario that runs once each evening.
detail: In Make, create a new scenario, your second and last on the free plan. Set it to run on a schedule, once a day in the evening.
Add an HTTP module that reads today's orders from Supabase, using your Project URL and anon key. Ask your AI for the exact Supabase read for today's rows if you are stuck.
Add an Email module to send yourself the digest, mapping {{count}}, {{total}}, and the list of orders.
Turn it on and run it once to test.

sections.name: Go live
sections.note: Both scenarios on. The agent runs without you.

steps.title: Final test
steps.body: Confirm both halves work.
detail: Place a test order. As the customer, you get the polished confirmation within about 15 minutes.
Run the digest scenario once. As the owner, you get the day's summary.
Make sure both scenarios are switched on.

steps.title: Your agent is live
steps.body: Every order gets a personal reply, and you get a daily summary, hands free.
detail: Every customer gets a confirmation in your voice, automatically.
You get one clean digest each evening, read from your own orders.
There is no new URL, the agent runs behind the app and Supabase you already have. Tell your facilitator it is live.

sessions.title: Office hours, pitch deck
sessions.set_the_stage:
Last office hours. Demo Day is one week away.

This is the session where your micro-venture becomes a brand. You walked out of the weekend with an app, a website, and an audio-video. You spent three weeks adding a chatbot and an agent. Today the venture gets a name, a logo, and a pitch deck that ties it all together.

Four steps in ninety minutes. Pick your venture name. Design your logo. Answer six questions. Build the deck in Gamma. You walk out with the deck you will pitch on Demo Day.
agenda: First 15 min, pick a name for your venture. Three approaches. Five options. Pick the one that lands.
Next 20 min, design your logo in Canva. Pick a template, drop in your name, choose a colour.
Next 15 min, answer six questions in a Google Doc. These answers go straight into the deck prompt.
Last 40 min, paste the prompt into Gamma, get a six-slide deck, upload your logo, tweak slides.

stuck_points.title: Cannot decide on a name
stuck_points.resolution: Three approaches. Person name (your name + what you do): 'Padma's Tutoring', 'Karthik Curates'. Place name (where it lives): 'Indiranagar Tiffin', 'Bagru Block'. Feeling name (what it gives the customer): 'Fresh Friday', 'Slow Plants'. Write 5. Read aloud. Pick the one that makes you smile.

stuck_points.title: Logo looking generic
stuck_points.resolution: In Canva, filter logo templates by SIMPLE or MINIMAL. Avoid templates with too many elements. One symbol or one wordmark is enough. Pick a colour that matches your venture's feeling, not a colour from the template gallery.

stuck_points.title: Gamma deck looks corporate
stuck_points.resolution: In the Gamma prompt, ask explicitly for 'warm colours, big visuals, short text, the kind of deck a founder presents to early customers and friends, not a startup investor.' If the first generation looks corporate, regenerate with that line emphasised.

stuck_points.title: Six questions feel like homework
stuck_points.resolution: They are not homework. They are the deck. The Gamma prompt is a template that pours your six answers into a six-slide deck in one shot. Write each answer in two short lines. Done in fifteen minutes.

prompts.title: Step 1: Pick your venture name (15 min)
prompts.content:
Spend 15 minutes brainstorming a name for YOUR specific venture. Not the generic idea name (Tiffin Choice, Rent a Tree etc). A name only you would pick.

Three ways to find a name:

1. The PERSON name approach: Your name + what you do. 'Padma's Tiffin', 'Karthik Curates', 'Anjali Bakes'.

2. The PLACE name approach: Where it lives. 'BalconyBuds', 'Indiranagar Tiffin', 'Bagru Block'.

3. The FEELING name approach: What it gives the customer. 'Fresh Friday', 'Slow Plants', 'Steady Mornings'.

Write 5 options. Read them aloud. Pick the one that makes you smile. That is your venture name.

prompts.title: Step 2: Design your logo in Canva (20 min)
prompts.content:
Open Canva. Free account.

1. Search 'logo' in the Canva templates.
2. Filter by SIMPLE / MINIMAL designs. Avoid templates with too many elements.
3. Replace the placeholder name with YOUR venture name from Step 1.
4. Pick a colour that matches your venture's feeling. Warm orange for plants. Mustard yellow for food. Deep green for farms. Pink for events.
5. Download as PNG with transparent background.

Save it. You will use it in the deck now and on your website later this week.

prompts.title: Step 3: Answer six questions (15 min)
prompts.content:
Answer these six questions in 2 lines each. Write them in a Google Doc. These answers go straight into your deck prompt.

1. WHO ARE YOU? Your name, your college, your year.

2. WHAT IS YOUR VENTURE CALLED? The name from Step 1.

3. WHAT IS YOUR ONE-LINE TAGLINE? What your venture does in 7 words or less. Example: 'Rent a tree. Eat the fruit for a season.'

4. WHO IS YOUR CUSTOMER? The real person who paid (or said they would pay). Name them. Where they live. What they do.

5. WHAT PROBLEM DID YOU SOLVE FOR THEM? In one sentence.

6. WHAT IS ONE REAL VALIDATION INSIGHT? What is one thing a real user said or did that proved the venture works? Quote them if you can.

prompts.title: Step 4: Build the deck in Gamma (40 min)
prompts.content:
Open gamma.app. Free tier gives you 400 credits. One deck takes 40 credits. Plenty.

Paste this prompt into Gamma. Replace the bracketed bits with YOUR answers from Step 3.

---

Build me a 6-slide pitch deck for my college micro-venture. I am a final-year engineering student.

[ANSWER 1: WHO ARE YOU]

MY VENTURE IS CALLED: [ANSWER 2]

TAGLINE: [ANSWER 3]

MY CUSTOMER: [ANSWER 4]

PROBLEM I SOLVED: [ANSWER 5]

REAL VALIDATION INSIGHT: [ANSWER 6]

My app is live at: [YOUR APP URL]
My website is live at: [YOUR WEBSITE URL]

Make the deck warm, simple, and visual. Not corporate. Six slides:

1. TITLE: My name, my venture name, my tagline. Reserve space for me to upload my logo.
2. THE PROBLEM: Who has it, the real customer from above, one specific example.
3. THE INSIGHT: What real user behaviour or quote proved the venture works.
4. WHAT I BUILT: Show that I have an app, a website, a chatbot, and an agent. Add URLs.
5. HOW IT WORKS: Three simple steps the customer takes.
6. WHAT COULD HAPPEN NEXT: If 20 paying customers used this, what would change?

Use warm colours. Big visuals. Short text. The kind of deck a founder would present at Demo Day to early customers, friends, and faculty, not to a VC.

build.title: The four-step arc
build.instructions: 15 min name. 20 min logo. 15 min answering questions. 40 min building the deck in Gamma. Stay with the clock.

build.title: Upload your logo into Gamma
build.instructions: Once Gamma generates the deck, drag your logo PNG from Canva onto slide 1. Place it next to your venture name.

build.title: Tweak the slides Gamma generated
build.instructions: Gamma usually nails 4 of 6 slides on the first try. Read each slide aloud. Fix the two that feel off. Do not regenerate the whole deck.

build.title: Leave with one polish
build.instructions: By the end of the call, every founder has one specific thing to fix on the deck before Demo Day. Not three. One. Specific.

test_output.title: The deck-on-projector test
test_output.description: If you screen-shared your deck on the call today and walked through the six slides in under three minutes, you are pitch-ready for Demo Day.

sessions.live_qa_note: Most founders over-engineer the deck. Six slides. Short text. Big visuals. The Gamma defaults are usually right. Do not spend the last 20 minutes choosing fonts.
sessions.homework_check: Every founder leaves with: a Gamma deck URL with all six slides filled in, their venture logo placed on slide 1, and one specific thing they will polish before Demo Day.

output_capture.label: Your pitch deck
output_capture.description: Save the Gamma share URL. This is the deck you present on Demo Day next Saturday.

sessions.title: Demo Day
sessions.set_the_stage:
Look at this room. It looks different from the morning of Day 1. That is intentional.

Five weeks ago you walked in with an idea on a card. You leave tonight with six live builds, collected into one URL, and a three-minute recording of yourself standing in this room demonstrating what you made to a room of strangers who had no reason to be impressed.

That recording becomes the most valuable thing in your portfolio. Recruiters do not read resumes anymore. They watch ninety seconds of footage and decide.

Demos this evening. Three minutes plus one minute of Q&A. Hard cap. We record every one. By the end of tonight every one of you has a Demo Day recording on your portfolio URL.

questions.title: What is your one specific ask?
questions.body: Every demo ends with one specific ask of the room. Not 'thank you'. Something the room can do tonight. Try the URL. Forward it to one person. Connect on LinkedIn. Send it to their parent or relative if relevant. Write your ask down before you pitch.

build.title: The four-beat demo
build.instructions: Beat 1: The problem and the person. Thirty seconds. Beat 2: The product, live on screen, with a real input. Ninety seconds. Beat 3: The one thing a real user said that you did not expect. Thirty seconds. Beat 4: The specific ask. Thirty seconds.

build.title: The Q&A minute
build.instructions: One minute for questions from the room. If no one asks, the facilitator asks. If you do not know the answer, say so. 'I do not know yet, but here is how I would find out' is a stronger answer than a guess.

build.title: Record everything
build.instructions: Camera on a tripod at the back of the room. Every demo recorded. Files uploaded the same night. Demo Day recording lives at the top of every student's portfolio URL by midnight.

test_output.title: The cap test
test_output.description: Did every demo come in under three minutes? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The recording test
test_output.description: Is every demo recorded, with audio that can be heard, and the screen capture clearly showing the AI app run? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The ask test
test_output.description: Did every demo end with one specific ask? If yes, pass.

output_capture.ask: Paste the link to the published Demo Day recording on your portfolio.
output_capture.guidance: The recording lives at the top of your portfolio URL. It plays automatically when someone opens the page. The recording is the strongest thing in the portfolio. Recruiters watch the demo, then explore the rest.

sessions.title: Close, what comes next
sessions.set_the_stage:
Stop for a moment. Look at the URL on your phone. Six weeks ago this did not exist. Not the product, not the website, not the chatbot, not the audio, not the video, not the agent, not the Demo Day recording.

You came in with an idea on a card. You leave with seven live things, collected into one URL.

This last block is about what you do with that URL across the next twelve months. Placements. Off-campus drives. Internship applications. Higher studies essays. LinkedIn that actually pulls recruiters to you. Your portfolio is the proof you can already do AI work. Use it everywhere.

At the very end, for those who want it, we open one more door. Orange Caterpillar Ventures for builders who want to keep building. Selective.

questions.title: What is the one thing you will do tonight with this URL?
questions.body: Before you sleep. One specific action. Update your LinkedIn featured section. Update your resume. Send the URL to one senior. Pin it to your WhatsApp status. The portfolio is only as valuable as the number of people who see it.

questions.title: What is the one thing you will do this week?
questions.body: Within seven days. Three specific people you will send this URL to, by name. A senior in your dream company. A college alum on LinkedIn. A professor or mentor. Write the three names down now. The portfolio works when it travels.

questions.title: What is the one thing you will do this month?
questions.body: Within thirty days. Apply to one off-campus drive. Apply to one internship through Internshala or LinkedIn. Reach out to one alum on LinkedIn for a fifteen-minute conversation. Walk into your college placement cell and show them your portfolio. Pick one and put it on the calendar.

techniques: Your portfolio is a long-running asset. Update it every time you build something new.
Personal messages beat generic applications. One DM with your portfolio URL to a senior at your dream company outperforms ten cold emails to careers@ inboxes.
If anyone responds, even with one line, reply within twelve hours. Speed signals seriousness.
Put the portfolio URL on your LinkedIn featured section, your resume, your Naukri profile, your Internshala profile, and your college placement portal. Five places, today.
Every change to your portfolio should be traceable to feedback. Not your own taste.
When you feel the urge to rebuild before sending, ask. Am I rebuilding because it is wrong, or because I am uncomfortable showing something imperfect?
Today. LinkedIn post drafted. Three people you will message identified.
Within 48 hours. Post published. Messages sent.
Next month. Apply to one off-campus drive or one internship where your strongest build is the proof. Walk into your placement cell and show them.
Placement season. Open your portfolio in the interview. Run the ninety-second demo from your phone.

build.title: Update LinkedIn now
build.instructions: Featured section: pin your Demo Day recording. About section: one sentence ending in your portfolio URL. Skills: add 'AI product development' if it is not there. Five minutes. Do it before you leave the room.

build.title: Update your resume now
build.instructions: Add one line under 'projects' or 'portfolio'. 'Built and shipped six AI products as a solopreneur in the Orange Caterpillar Just Build cohort. Portfolio: yourname.justbuild.orangecaterpillar.com'. Five minutes.

build.title: Send three messages tonight
build.instructions: Three people, before you sleep. A senior at a company you would like to work for. An alum from your college on LinkedIn. A professor or mentor who has shown interest in your work. WhatsApp or DM. One line: 'Spent six weeks building this, thought you might want to see.' Plus the URL. Speed signals seriousness.

test_output.title: The LinkedIn test
test_output.description: Is your Demo Day recording pinned to your LinkedIn featured section before you leave this room? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The resume test
test_output.description: Is your portfolio URL on your resume, in the projects section, before you leave this room? If yes, pass.

test_output.title: The three-messages test
test_output.description: Will you send the three messages before you sleep tonight? If yes, pass.

ideas.name: Rent a Tree
ideas.for_whom: You, if your family has a farm, or a relative who has one
ideas.problem: Small farmers earn 30% of retail; middlemen control distribution. Urban families pay premium for fruit but cannot find a trusted direct source.
ideas.what_app_does: Shows a catalogue of trees with photos and the farmer's story. Customer picks a tree, pays for the season, sees updates over months. Produce delivers when ready.

ideas.name: Tiffin Choice
ideas.for_whom: You, if you cook well and could feed 30 office-goers nearby
ideas.problem: Customers do not know the weekly menu in advance. The cook does not know who wants what until she has already cooked.
ideas.what_app_does: Shows the upcoming weekly menu. Lets regular customers pick three preferred dishes for next week. Sends preferences to the cook.

ideas.name: Mehendi Bookings
ideas.for_whom: You, if you do mehendi, or a friend who does it for events
ideas.problem: Brides spend a week messaging five artists on Instagram. The artist who responds slowly loses the booking.
ideas.what_app_does: Shows the artist's portfolio by style. Customers see pricing per hand or design, check availability, book directly. Booking arrives on WhatsApp.

ideas.name: Makeup Enquiry
ideas.for_whom: You, if you do makeup well, or a friend who freelances
ideas.problem: Bridal makeup is a 15K to 75K booking. Brides spend a week comparing artists on Instagram and never get structured information.
ideas.what_app_does: Portfolio organised by occasion. Brides answer a guided questionnaire (event, venue, looks, allergies). Complete enquiry arrives on the artist's WhatsApp.

ideas.name: Jewellery Curator
ideas.for_whom: You, if you have access to a goldsmith, lac-maker, or silver craftsman
ideas.problem: Indian buyers shifting from showrooms to online want curated pieces with stories, not generic catalogues.
ideas.what_app_does: Collection by occasion and style. Each piece has the maker's story, weight, and care instructions. Custom orders go to the goldsmith via a guided form.

ideas.name: Alteration Service
ideas.for_whom: You, if you have access to a tailor, or your mother sews well
ideas.problem: Working women buy clothes online and most need small alterations. Local tailors do not do pickup-and-delivery.
ideas.what_app_does: Customers browse pricing, book a pickup slot, courier the piece with reference photos. The tailor returns it altered in four days.

ideas.name: Exam Prep
ideas.for_whom: You, if you are strong in one school subject
ideas.problem: Group tuitions are generic. Online platforms like Vedantu cost 50K-2L and are too broad.
ideas.what_app_does: Coach's specialisation, a 12-week or 24-week program structure, recent students' results. Parents see the plan, book a trial class, subscribe monthly.

ideas.name: Senior Mornings
ideas.for_whom: You, if you have an elderly parent or grandparent looking for structure
ideas.problem: Active retirees face a vacuum after retirement. Existing wellness apps target younger users. Joint-family support has shrunk.
ideas.what_app_does: One short reading or video daily, one practical task, a gentle log of yesterday. Family can check if the senior opened it today. Content pre-written, rotates by date.

ideas.name: Neighbourhood Bakery
ideas.for_whom: You, if you bake well, or a relative who already bakes for friends
ideas.problem: Demand grows beyond what WhatsApp can handle. Custom orders for birthdays and festivals get lost in chat history.
ideas.what_app_does: Bake catalogue with photos and prices. Custom orders use a structured form. Confirmed orders go to the baker as a clean WhatsApp message.

ideas.name: Tutoring Slots
ideas.for_whom: You, if you tutor younger students (Class 5-10), or a friend who does
ideas.problem: Parents make many calls to book a tutor. The tutor wastes evenings confirming slots over WhatsApp.
ideas.what_app_does: Tutor's subjects, sample lesson topics, available time slots, recent students' progress. Parents pick a free trial slot. Booking confirmed in person.

ideas.name: Plant Catalogue
ideas.for_whom: You, if you grow plants at home, or a neighbour aunty who runs a nursery
ideas.problem: Apartment dwellers want plants but kill them because they pick wrong for their light conditions.
ideas.what_app_does: Available plants with photos, prices, care difficulty (easy / moderate / demanding). Customers pick by their light, order, pay. Plants delivered by the founder.

ideas.name: Direct Dairy
ideas.for_whom: You, if your family has a small dairy farm, or a relative does
ideas.problem: Urban families pay 80-120/L for branded milk that is industrial dairy in fancy packaging. The farmer sells to wholesalers for 40/L.
ideas.what_app_does: Shows the farm and the cows. Customers subscribe to weekly home delivery of milk, paneer, ghee. Delivery on a fixed weekly route the farmer optimises.

ideas.name: Handicraft Brand
ideas.for_whom: You, if you have visited one craft cluster (Channapatna, Moradabad, Molela)
ideas.problem: Urban Indians want Indian-aesthetic decor. FabIndia is sterile and IKEA does not get the language.
ideas.what_app_does: Curated decor pieces with stories about the cluster and the artisan. New pieces drop monthly. Customers order, founder ships.

ideas.name: Hyperlocal Wash
ideas.for_whom: You, if you can hire two boys and serve one apartment complex
ideas.problem: Urban Indians spend 1K-2.5K/month getting their car washed badly. Reliable hyperlocal service does not exist at apartment-complex scale.
ideas.what_app_does: Residents of one complex subscribe monthly. Shows the wash schedule for the week. After each wash, residents see 'your car is done' on WhatsApp.

ideas.name: Two-Wheeler Service
ideas.for_whom: You, if you have a relative who runs a bike or scooter repair shop
ideas.problem: Customers drop their bike at the service shop and have no clarity on what was done or what it cost.
ideas.what_app_does: Customers fill in what is wrong and how urgent. Mechanic logs work done with photos. Customers get a clean bill on WhatsApp before pickup.

ideas.name: Pre-Loved Kids
ideas.for_whom: You, if you have access to many parents in your building or college network
ideas.problem: Kids outgrow good clothes in three months. Parents spend 30K-80K/year on briefly-used clothes.
ideas.what_app_does: Curated pre-loved kids clothes by size and brand. Sellers list pieces, founder verifies and ships. Buyers order, founder takes a margin.

ideas.name: Pet Sitter
ideas.for_whom: You, if you love pets and have time on weekends
ideas.problem: Working couples with dogs and cats struggle to find someone trustworthy and nearby to walk or feed their pet.
ideas.what_app_does: Sitter's profile and availability for the week. Pet owners book slots and pay. After each visit, the sitter sends a photo update on WhatsApp.

ideas.name: Fest Photographer
ideas.for_whom: You, if you do photography as a hobby and own a decent camera
ideas.problem: College fest organisers hire expensive professionals or whoever they know. No reliable middle option for student photographers.
ideas.what_app_does: Photographer's portfolio, packages (raw photos, edited highlights, reels), weekend availability. Fest organisers see pricing upfront and book.

ideas.name: Saree Curator
ideas.for_whom: You, if you have taste in sarees and a wholesaler contact
ideas.problem: Working women want curated sarees but online shops show thousands of generic options. Instagram saree sellers are unreliable.
ideas.what_app_does: Curated collection by region, occasion, or weave. Each saree has the weaver's story, fabric details, styling suggestions. Customers order, founder ships.

ideas.name: Block Print Brand
ideas.for_whom: You, if you have visited Bagru, Sanganer, or another block-printing cluster
ideas.problem: Urban buyers want authentic block prints but FabIndia is corporate and Instagram resellers are unreliable.
ideas.what_app_does: Curated collection of block-printed clothes and home goods. Each piece tells the story of the cluster and the printer. New drops monthly.

ideas.name: Home Pickle
ideas.for_whom: You, if your mother or grandmother makes legendary pickles
ideas.problem: Industrial pickles have killed the taste people remember from home. Urban Indians cannot find a small home-maker they trust.
ideas.what_app_does: Product range with stories about recipes and ingredients. Customers order monthly boxes or one-off jars. Home-cook ships in courier-friendly packaging.

ideas.name: Festival Catering
ideas.for_whom: You, if you or your family cook well for groups
ideas.problem: Big caterers refuse functions below 50 people. Hosts of small birthdays, naming ceremonies, satsangs have nowhere to turn at 300-500/head.
ideas.what_app_does: Menus by function type at different per-head prices. Hosts pick, specify dietary needs, lock the date. Order arrives on WhatsApp.

ideas.name: Poultry Direct
ideas.for_whom: You, if your family raises poultry, or a relative does
ideas.problem: Urban families pay 80-120/dozen for 'free-range' eggs they cannot verify. The farmer sells to wholesalers for 30.
ideas.what_app_does: Shows the farm and the chickens. Customers subscribe to weekly or biweekly egg deliveries. Delivery on a fixed route the farmer plans weekly.

ideas.name: Senior Travel
ideas.for_whom: You, if you have an elderly relative who travels, or you like planning trips
ideas.problem: Indian seniors have travel budgets but SOTC and Thomas Cook treat them as generic tourists. Trusted senior-paced trips are hard to find.
ideas.what_app_does: Curated small-group senior tours (pilgrimage, heritage, hill stations). Travellers browse, book, pay. Founder handles all logistics end-to-end.
